request.question: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?

title: Pippa Passes, Kentucky
Saluta Rocks
10 Downing Street
Uriconian
On the Rocks (play)
Snag Rocks
Ramp Rocks
Recreation room
Battle of Muhu
Drawing room play
sentences: Pippa Passes is a home rule-class city located along Caney Creek in Knott County, eastern Kentucky, United States.
Its formal name was chosen to honor benefactors of Alice Lloyd College.
After more than a century, its residents commonly call the community as Caney or Caney Creek.
The population was 533 at the time of the year 2010 U.S. Census.
The small city is located in the mountainous Appalachia region, an area of coal mining.
Saluta Rocks ( ) is a group of rocks 1 nautical mile (1.9 km) east of Laurie Point, lying off the south coast and near the west end of South Georgia.
The name Mutt and Jeff was probably given by Lieutenant Commander J.M. Chaplin of the 1926.
The SGS, 1955–56, reported that the name is misleading; there are not two rocks as implied, but a group.
The rocks were renamed by the United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) for the Saluta, a transport of the South Georgia Whaling Co.
for many years.

10 Downing Street, colloquially known in the United Kingdom as Number 10, is the headquarters of the Government of the United Kingdom and the official residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury, a post which, for much of the 18th and 19th centuries and invariably since 1905, has been held by the Prime Minister.
The Uriconian rocks of Shropshire (Wrekin Terrane) are thought to be potentially related to the Longmyndian Supergroup of the Stretton Hills, Shropshire, United Kingdom.
Current geological profiling of the terranes suggests that the Uriconian rocks are of Precambrian age (Neoproterozoic Phases 2 and 3).
The Uriconian Rocks outcrop to the southeast of the Long Mynd area of the Welsh Borderland Fault System and beyond the Church Stretton Fault which trends northeast-southwest across the area.
The Stretton Hills are composed primarily of arenaceous (quartz rich sand) beds assigned to the Longmyndian Supergroup.
The Longmyndian rocks are a c.6 km thick group of volcaniclastic and bentonitic sediment horizons.

The Uriconian have long thought to be older than the Longmyndian and generally outcrop to the southeast of the latter within northeast-southwest trending lineaments suggesting basement influence for the regional structure.
On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.
The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.
It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.
Snag Rocks, ( ) also known as Rocas Bravo, is a cluster of rocks off the west coast of the Antarctic Peninsula.
This cluster of rocks lies mid-channel in the French Passage between the Roca Islands and the Myriad Islands, in the Wilhelm Archipelago.

Photographed from the helicopter of HMS Protector in March 1958, Snag Rocks was so named by United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) because the feature presents a hazard or obstacle to navigation.
Ramp Rocks ( ) are three barren rocks, the largest being 23 m high, lying 2.5 nautical miles (4.6 km) northwest of Johannesen Point, Main Island, at the west extremity of South Georgia.
The name "Laavebrua," a descriptive Norwegian term meaning literally "threshing floor bridge" or "barn bridge," was used for the largest rock by whalers and sealers at South Georgia.
In Norwegian barns used for storing hay, there is a ramp up which the wagons are driven before tipping.
"Laavebrua," which is not strictly translatable, is this ramp.
The United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) recommended in 1954 that "Ramp Rock" be approved for the large rock, but in 1976 altered the name to Ramp Rocks to include the three rocks.
The name Låvebrua Island is already approved for an island off Deception Island.
A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.
The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.
Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.
Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.
In January 1227, when the sea had frozen hard, a large Crusader army after the call of the Papal legate William of Modena was gathered.
According to the Chronicle of Livonia, 20 000 troops had set out to Muhu from the estuary of Pärnu River and moved along the ice.
Since the ice was said to have been very slippery, they reached the stronghold of Muhu on the ninth day.
After seeing such a large force, the people of Muhu offered truce and allowed themselves to be baptised.
Most of the Germans didn't accept it and so they started besieging the stronghold.
The first rush was fend off with rocks and spears.
The besiegers threw rocks into the stronghold with bricoles, built a turret and undermined the wall.

A drawing room play is a type of play, developed during the Victorian period in the United Kingdom, in which the actions take place in a drawing room or which is designed to be reenacted in the drawing room of a home.

The common practice of entertaining a guest in the home led to the creation of this category of plays and while the drawing room itself has fallen out of favour, the play format has continued to provide a source of entertainment.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?
hop1_docs: Eze-Igwe | The Oguta kingdom was established in like manners to the Benin Kingdom of Nigeria.  Many people in Oguta believe that the Oguta people migrated from the ancient Benin Kingdom.  They argue that the administrative set up, titles and common names have similarities to those of Bini people.  Although the people of Oguta do not call their king Oba as the Bini do, both kingdoms have some striking parallels.  The Binis have Iyase, in Oguta it is called Iyasara, there is Ogine among the Bini and Ogana in Oguta.  Palm oil and plantain are called ofigbo and ipa in both Edo language and Oguta dialect to this day.  Although the Oguta people speak a dialect of the Igbo language, some of their words are unmistakably of Bini origin.

Recreation room | A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.  The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.  Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.  Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.

Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song) | "Do What You Do" is a single by American R&B singer Jermaine Jackson, sibling of singers Michael and Janet Jackson and former member of The Jackson 5.  It was released as the second single his album, which was called "Jermaine Jackson" in the United States and "Dynamite" in the United Kingdom and other countries.
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do | "Hey, Hey, What Can I Do" is a song by the English rock group Led Zeppelin, released in 1970 as the B-side of "Immigrant Song" outside the United Kingdom.

On the Rocks (play) | On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.  The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.  It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.

That's What People Do | That's What People Do is an independently released album by the rock band Thousand Foot Krutch.  Rhyme Animal is the first and only single from this album, released in Canada.  The tracks titled "Rhyme Animal", "Small Town", "Lift It" and "Set It Off" were rerecorded in 2000 for the album Set It Off and "Rhyme Animal" was re-titled "Rhime Animal".  This is the only TFK album to have no music videos, until the 2009 record "Welcome to the Masquerade".  Trevor McNevan is the only member on the album who is still in the band.

Big Tent Revival | Big Tent Revival is a Christian rock band that formed in 1991, toured extensively, disbanded in 2000, and reformed in 2012.  They were featured at the Harvest Crusades.  Their most popular songs were "Two Sets of Jones'", "Choose Life", and "What Would Jesus Do?" .  The first told a story about two different couples in which one trusted in Jesus through the storms of life and the other didn't.  The second was used as an invitational at Harvest Crusade altar calls.  The last was part of the WWJD movement that encouraged people to consider what Jesus would do in real-life situations.
hop1_titles: Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song)
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found: "On the Rocks (play)" passage [5] states the play is "set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street" (quote: "Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street" — On the Rocks (play), passage [5]). No passage here gives a UK colloquial name for that location.
- Ambiguities: The passages do not present multiple candidate pages for the location—only "Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street" appears. A page/variant explicitly giving the British/common spoken name (e.g., whether people call it "the Cabinet Room", "Number 10", "Downing Street", etc.) is missing from the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact: Bridge entity = "Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street" (exact phrase from passage [5]). Missing fact needed = the precise term people in the United Kingdom commonly use to refer to that location (the colloquial/common name).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Cabinet Room" page (or "Cabinet Room, 10 Downing Street") — check lede/infobox and sections about naming/usage or "Location" for common/colloquial names.
     - Search keywords: Cabinet Room 10 Downing Street "commonly called" "also known as"
  2) Open the "10 Downing Street" page — check lede, "Number 10" aliases, "Interior" or "Rooms" sections for how the Cabinet Room is referred to colloquially.
     - Search keywords: "10 Downing Street" Cabinet Room common name "Number 10"
  3) If needed, open "Cabinet of the United Kingdom" or "Cabinet Office (United Kingdom)" pages and check for references to "Cabinet Room" naming conventions.
  4) If there is a disambiguation, open "Cabinet Room (disambiguation)" or related pages to confirm variants.
Note: Do not answer the question until those pages/sections are retrieved and checked.
response.hop2_query: Cabinet Room 10 Downing Street common name
hop2_docs: On the Rocks (play) | On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.  The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.  It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.

Downing Street mortar attack | The Downing Street mortar attack was carried out by the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) on 7 February 1991.  The IRA launched homemade mortar shells at 10 Downing Street, London, the official residence of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.  It was an attempt to assassinate Prime Minister John Major and his War Cabinet, who were meeting to discuss the Gulf War.  One of the heavy mortar shells exploded in the back garden of number 10, only yards from the cabinet office.  Due to the bomb-proof windows, none of the cabinet were hurt, though four other people received minor injuries, including two police officers.  The other two shells overshot Downing Street and landed on a green nearby.

Parliamentary Secretary to the Treasury | The Parliamentary Secretary to the Treasury is a junior ministerial position in the British Government.  However, the office is now attached to the Treasury in name only.  The holder is usually the Government Chief Whip in the House of Commons.  The office can be seen as a sinecure, allowing the Chief Whip to draw a government salary, attend Cabinet, and use a Downing Street residence.

Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office | The Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office is the title of the official resident cat of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom at 10 Downing Street.  Only four cats, Humphrey, Sybil, Larry and Freya have been given the title officially; other cats were given this title affectionately, usually by the British press.  There has been a resident Treasury or Downing Street cat employed as a mouser and pet since the reign of Henry VIII, when Cardinal Wolsey placed his cat by his side while acting in his judicial capacity as Lord Chancellor, an office he assumed in 1515.

Richard Pim | Captain Sir Richard Pike Pim, KBE, VRD, DL, RNVR (10 July 1900 - 26 June 1987) was a British civil servant and naval officer.  He was the Inspector-General of the Royal Ulster Constabulary from August 1945 to January 1961.  During World War II he served as supervisor of the Defence Map Room, 10 Downing Street and head of the Upper War Room, Admiralty.

Chief Whip | The Chief Whip is a political office in some legislatures whose task is to administer the whipping system that tries to ensure that members of the party attend and vote as the party leadership desires.  In British politics, the Chief Whip of the governing party in the House of Commons is usually also appointed as Parliamentary Secretary to the Treasury, a Cabinet position.  The Government Chief Whip has an official residence at 12 Downing Street.  However, the Chief Whip's office is currently located at 9 Downing Street.

Larry (cat) | Larry is the 10 Downing Street cat and is Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office.  Larry is a brown and white tabby, believed to have been born in January 2007.  By July 2016, when Theresa May became Prime Minister, Larry had developed a reputation of being "violent" in his interactions with other local mousers, namely the Foreign Office's much younger cat Palmerston.
hop2_titles: Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office
response.summary_2: The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street. 10 Downing Street is the official residence of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, and the passages do not specify a common UK colloquial name for the Cabinet Room.
response.answer: No common colloquial name specified in the provided summaries.
